title: Global adiabatic criterion for fast topological photon transfer in Fock-state lattices
authors: Jin-Lei Wu, Pei-Yao Song, Jia Li, Ya Gao, Yan Wang, Shi-Lei Su
abstract: Topological state transfer in Fock-state lattices has been demonstrated with high speed using sinusoidal profiles of coupling, yet the underlying reason has remained unclear. A global adiabatic criterion (GAC) is developed to bound the infidelity by the mean and variance of the nonadiabatic factor. The GAC reveals that the key to fast transfer is not a constant energy gap but the vanishing nonadiabaticity variance. For power-law coupling profiles, the variance vanishes only for the sinusoidal shape, which is thus globally optimal. Incorporating experimental decoherence parameters, it is predicted that the optimal transfer duration for a five-photon state is 161 ns, far shorter than 600 ns used in the experiment, reducing time by over 73% while increasing transferred photons by 29%. The optimal duration follow a simple linear scaling with photon number, providing a practical guideline. Through constructing an alternative constant-gap coupling family, it is confirmed that a constant gap alone is not sufficient for fast topological photon transfer. The essential condition is uniformity of nonadiabaticity. This work offers a rigorous explanation for the observed speed and a general framework for fast topological photonics engineering.
summary: This paper develops a Global Adiabatic Criterion (GAC) to explain fast topological photon transfer in Fock-state lattices. It proves that the key to speed is minimizing the variance of the nonadiabatic coupling, not just maintaining a constant energy gap. The optimal protocol uses a sinusoidal coupling profile, predicting significantly faster and more efficient transfer times than previously reported.
main_problem: To provide a rigorous explanation for the fast topological photon transfer observed in Fock-state lattices and establish a general framework for fast topological photonics engineering.
main_result: The essential condition for fast transfer is the vanishing temporal variance of the nonadiabatic coupling factor ($\sigma_Q^2 = 0$), which is achieved optimally using a sinusoidal coupling profile ($\alpha=1$).
method: The authors developed a Global Adiabatic Criterion (GAC) to bound infidelity using the mean and variance of the nonadiabatic factor, analyzing power-law coupling profiles.
model_or_system: The system is modeled using a two-mode Jaynes-Cummings (JC) model applied to Fock-state lattices (FSLs), which simulates topological photon transfer.
key_observables: Transferred photon number ($ ar{n}_2$), infidelity ($\epsilon$), and the nonadiabaticity variance ($\sigma_Q^2$).
important_parameters: Optimal transfer duration for a five-photon state is predicted to be 161 ns, showing a linear scaling with photon number $N$.
main_assumptions: The analysis incorporates experimental decoherence parameters and relies on the validity of the Global Adiabatic Criterion.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the setup (JC model, FSL), compare fidelity/photon number vs. coupling exponent ($\alpha$), and show the variance $\sigma_Q^2$ vanishing only for the sinusoidal case ($\alpha=1$).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical model and the GAC, analyzes power-law coupling profiles, determines the optimal profile by minimizing nonadiabaticity variance, and finally predicts significantly faster transfer times compared to previous experimental benchmarks.
why_interesting: This work provides a quantitative, general theoretical framework for optimizing quantum light transport, moving beyond simple gap-closing arguments to focus on the uniformity of nonadiabatic dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Topological photon transfer in a two-mode JC model. (a) Schematic of the two-mode JC model: two cavity modes (resonators) are coupled to a common spin (qubit) with time-dependent strengths g1(t) and g2(t). (b) Superconducting circuit implementation of the device (adapted from Ref. [25]), consisting of a central gmon qubit inductively coupled to two resonators via tunable couplers.
